channel.title: Nicotine Science and Policy
channel.description: Daily digest of views, debates and discussions on nicotine science, policy, regulation and advocacy.

item.title: David Clement: Banning flavoured vapes harms adults and does little for youth
item.description: Flavour bans sound simple, but their effects are not. For many adults, flavours help make vaping a more acceptable substitute for cigarettes — and removing them may push people back towards smoking or into unregulated markets. Protecting young people requires strong enforcement and responsible retail controls, not policies that remove useful options for adults trying to quit. A ban that weakens switching while doing little to stop youth access is not good public health. (Published on July 13, 2026bynationalpost.com)

item.title: Who Benefits from the Status Quo? - Part 3 Final
item.description: When tobacco policy preserves the status quo, the most harmful product remains protected. Cigarettes stay familiar, available and profitable, while lower-risk alternatives face suspicion, restriction and misinformation. The result is a system that talks about reducing smoking but often makes switching harder in practice. Public health should be willing to challenge the incentives that keep combustion at the centre of the market. (Published on July 13, 2026bysubstack.com)

item.title: Regulators accused of stubbing out Sweden’s ‘solution’ to smoking
item.description: Sweden’s success in reducing smoking should be studied, not sidelined. By allowing adults access to smoke-free nicotine alternatives, the country has shown that tobacco control can move faster when policy reflects relative risk. If regulators ignore that lesson, they risk protecting outdated approaches while smokers elsewhere are denied more practical routes away from cigarettes. The goal should be less disease and fewer deaths — not loyalty to one narrow model of quitting. (Published on July 13, 2026byobserver.co.uk)

item.title: The False Claims That Keep People Smoking
item.description: False claims about smoke-free nicotine products do not make people safer — they can keep people smoking. When adults are told that lower-risk alternatives are just as harmful as cigarettes, confusion replaces informed choice and the incentive to switch weakens. Misinformation protects the product causing the greatest harm: combustible tobacco. Public health needs honesty about risk, not messages that make all nicotine products look the same. (Published on July 13, 2026bydaily-pouch.com)

item.title: Do smoke-free product/flavour bans work? | #GFN26 Workshop
item.description: Flavour bans promise simple solutions, but the real-world evidence is far less convincing. When smoke-free products become less appealing or harder to access, adult smokers may lose one of the reasons switching away from cigarettes works. Youth protection should be taken seriously, but policy must also consider unintended consequences: illicit markets, reduced switching and a return to smoking. A ban is not a success if it removes options without reducing harm. (Published on July 13, 2026bygfn.tv)
